description: city in North Carolina, United States of America
description_wiki: Greensboro ( ; locally ) is a city in Guilford County, North Carolina, United States, and its county seat.

entity: North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University
aliases: North Carolina Agricultural & Technical State University
description: university
American retail company
American 1960s civil rights campaign
description_wiki: The sit-in movement, sit-in campaign, or student sit-in movement, was a wave of sit-ins that followed the Greensboro sit-ins on February 1, 1960, led by students – Ezell Blair Jr., David Richmond, Franklin McCain, and Joseph McNeil – at North Carolina Agricultural and Technical Institute (A&T).

description: capital city of Georgia, United States
description_wiki: Atlanta is the capital and most populous city of the U.S. state of Georgia.

description: largest student-led civil rights organization during the American Civil Rights Movement
description_wiki: The Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, and later the Student National Coordinating Committee (SNCC, pronounced  SNIK), was the principal channel of student commitment in the United States to the civil rights movement during the 1960s.

description: K-12 laboratory school in Baton Rouge, Louisiana
description_wiki: Southern University Laboratory School (Southern Lab or SULAB or SULS) is a K-12 laboratory school in Baton Rouge, Louisiana, affiliated with Southern University.

description: college located in Baton Rouge, Louisiana
description_wiki: Southern University and A&M College (Southern University, Southern, SUBR or SU) is a public historically black land-grant university in Baton Rouge, Louisiana, United States.

description: public research university in Philadelphia, United States
description_wiki: Temple University (Temple or TU) is a public state-related research university in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, United States.

description: largest city in Pennsylvania, United States
description_wiki: Philadelphia (  FIL-ə-DEL-fee-ə), colloquially referred to as Philly, is the most populous city in the U.S. state of Pennsylvania, and the sixth-most populous city in the United States, with a Census-estimated population of 1,574,281 in July 2025.

description: American activist and theologian (1940–2002)
description_wiki: Prathia Laura Ann Hall Wynn (January 1, 1940 – August 12, 2002) was an American leader and activist in the Civil Rights Movement, a womanist theologian, and ethicist.

description: state of the United States of America
description_wiki: North Carolina (  KAYR-ə-LIE-nə) is a state in the Southeastern and South Atlantic regions of the United States.

description: American white supremacist hate group
description_wiki: The Ku Klux Klan (KKK; ), sometimes referred to as the Klan, is an American Protestant-led white supremacist and far-right hate group.

description: 1939–1945 global conflict
description_wiki: World War II, or the Second World War (1 September 1939 – 2 September 1945), was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers.

aliases: War to Resist US Aggression and Aid Korea
description: war between North and South Korea, 1950–1953
description_wiki: The Korean War (25 June 1950 – 27 July 1953) was an armed conflict the Korean Peninsula fought between North Korea (Democratic People's Republic of Korea; DPRK) and South Korea (Republic of Korea; ROK) and their allies.

description: capital and most populous city of Russia
description_wiki: Moscow is the capital and largest city of Russia, standing on the Moskva River in Central Russia.

description: city in Russia, capital city of Chelyabinsk Oblast
description_wiki: Chelyabinsk is the administrative center and largest city of Chelyabinsk Oblast, Russia.

description: state and local laws enforcing racial segregation in the Southern United States
description_wiki: The Jim Crow laws were state and local laws introduced in the Southern United States in the late 19th and early-to-mid-20th centuries that enforced racial segregation.

description: 1960 non-violent protests in the United States
description_wiki: The Greensboro sit-ins were a series of nonviolent protests in February to July 1960, primarily in the Woolworth store—now the International Civil Rights Center and Museum—in Greensboro, North Carolina, which led to the F. W. Woolworth Company department store chain removing its policy of racial segregation in the Southern United States.

description: region in Georgia, United States
description_wiki: Southwest Georgia is a fourteen-county region in the U.S. state of Georgia, bordering Alabama and Florida.
